relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Herod hears the report of Jesus.
His fear interprets Jesus through the shadow of John.
John had been bound because he spoke truth to power.
His witness named unlawful desire.
Herod feared the crowd even while resisting the prophet.
A royal celebration becomes the setting for a deadly request.
A reckless oath binds Herod to performance before guests.
The request reveals the violence hidden in the palace.
Grief does not free Herod from the trap of his own oath.
John is killed in prison.
The prophet’s death is displayed by corrupt power.
John’s disciples bury him and report to Jesus.
Jesus withdraws alone, yet the crowds follow.
Compassion meets the crowd before teaching or feeding.
The disciples see lack and want to send the crowd away.
Jesus turns the need back into shared responsibility.
Five loaves and two fish are all they see.
Jesus asks that the little be brought to him.
He blesses, breaks, and gives through the disciples.
All are filled, and twelve baskets remain.
The feeding extends beyond the counted men to women and children.
Jesus sends the disciples across while releasing the crowd.
He goes up the mountain alone to pray.
The boat struggles against wind and waves.
In the deep night watch, Jesus comes walking on the sea.
Fear misreads his presence as an apparition.
He speaks courage: I am; do not fear.
Peter asks to come to him on the waters.
At the word Come, Peter steps onto the sea.
Seeing the wind, fear divides his trust and he cries for saving.
Jesus immediately takes his hand and names the divided trust.
When they enter the boat, the wind stops.
The boat becomes a place of worship and recognition.
They cross to Gennesaret.
Recognition spreads and the sick are brought.
Even touching the fringe becomes a channel of complete healing.
